title: Catskill Eagle
description: Catskill Eagle is a book by Robert B Parker. The exported reason says: May facilitate or encourage criminal activity; describes methods to commit a felony
descriptionParagraphs: Catskill Eagle is a book by Robert B Parker. The exported reason says: May facilitate or encourage criminal activity; describes methods to commit a felony

What makes it interesting is the prison-censorship logic: officials treat the book as a practical threat model and collapse the distinction between reading about something and doing it. As a book, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in Michigan. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: The Michigan prison-ban record treats the book as excluded reading inside state custody, which shows how prison and mailroom censorship function as a government reading regime. The exported reason says: May facilitate. More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

overview.englishDescription: Catskill Eagle is a book by Robert B Parker. The exported reason says: May facilitate or encourage criminal activity; describes methods to commit a felony
banningParagraphs: Catskill Eagle entered censorship debates as a book associated with circulation politics, institutional control, and risk knowledge. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around instructional harm and public order.

The earliest event currently captured here is 2012-07-06 in Michigan, where Michigan corrections agencies and prison mailrooms excluded from prison circulation. The Michigan prison-ban record treats the book as excluded reading inside state custody, which shows how prison and mailroom censorship function as a government reading regime. The exported reason says: May facilitate. The Michigan prison-ban record treats the book as excluded reading inside state custody, which shows how prison and mailroom censorship function as a government reading regime. The exported reason says: May facilitate or encourage criminal activity.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.
